candidate.title: Machine-readable conformance test vectors (JSON)
candidate.description: Produce 50+ JSON test vectors organized by circuit (HumanUniqueness, AgentPolicy, Delegation) and scenario: valid witness, expired credential (expiryTimestamp < currentTimestamp), revoked human nullifier, stale Merkle root not in history buffer, scope subset violation (delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope != 0), cumulative bit encoding violation (bit 4 without bits 2+3), delegation chain at depths 1/2/3/4 (4 must fail), and replay with reused nonce. Each vector specifies inputs, expected outputs, and whether the proof MUST succeed or MUST fail with a specific error code. This is the minimum bar for any second implementation to claim conformance.

candidate.title: Add delegatee Merkle inclusion proof to Delegation circuit
candidate.description: The Delegation circuit accepts delegateeCredCommitment as a private input but never proves it exists in any Merkle tree. An attacker can fabricate a credential commitment, receive a valid delegation, and use the resulting scopeCommitment downstream. Add a Merkle inclusion proof (reusing the BinaryMerkleRoot template at MAX_DEPTH=20) against the agentTree root, passed as a public input and checked on-chain against the root history buffer. This is the single highest-severity soundness bug in the current circuit set.

candidate.title: Write formal invariant properties for all three circuits
candidate.description: Define and machine-check the following properties using circom-compat tooling or a custom R1CS analyzer: (1) No unconstrained signals — every private input flows into at least one constraint that gates an output. (2) Nullifier uniqueness — for fixed (scope, secret) or (credentialCommitment, nonce), exactly one nullifier value satisfies the constraints. (3) Scope monotonicity — in Delegation, prove that for any satisfying witness, delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0 is enforced (no bit escalation). (4) Field overflow absence — all Num2Bits(64) decompositions fully constrain their input to [0, 2^64). Deliver as a test harness with property assertions that run in CI, using either ecne (R1CS constraint analyzer) or picus (underconstrained signal detector).

candidate.title: did:bolyra DID Method Specification with VC issuance
candidate.description: Define a W3C DID method `did:bolyra:<base58(identityCommitment)>` with a DID Document structure that includes a `BolyraVerification2024` verification method type pointing to the on-chain IdentityRegistry. Specify the resolution algorithm: given a DID, derive the identity commitment, query the humanTree or agentTree for membership, and return the DID Document with service endpoints. Define a Verifiable Credential schema for handshake results (issuer = verifier service, subject = did:bolyra:*, credentialSubject includes scopeCommitment and sessionNonce). This bridges Bolyra into the existing SSI ecosystem and enables interop with any W3C DID-compatible wallet or verifier.

candidate.title: Add explicit less-than constraint for expiry checks in AgentPolicy
candidate.description: AgentPolicy performs Num2Bits(64) range checks on expiryTimestamp and currentTimestamp but the circuit source shown is truncated and may lack an explicit LessThan(64) constraint proving currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp. If this comparison is missing or only checked in Solidity, an attacker can generate a proof with an expired credential and submit it before the contract-side check (or the contract may not re-derive expiry from public signals). Add a LessThan(64) component inside the circuit: isNotExpired.in[0] <== currentTimestamp; isNotExpired.in[1] <== expiryTimestamp; isNotExpired.out === 1. Similarly verify the Delegation circuit enforces delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry inside the SNARK, not just in the contract. Constraint cost is ~130 per LessThan(64).

candidate.title: Add root history buffer for humanTree matching agentTree's 30-root buffer
candidate.description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree (agentRootHistory, agentRootExists) but humanTree has no equivalent. The humanMerkleRoot output from HumanUniqueness is verified against a live tree root, meaning any new enrollment between proof generation and on-chain verification invalidates the proof. Add a parallel uint256[30] humanRootHistory with the same rotation logic, update it in enrollHuman(), and check humanMerkleRoot against it in verifyHandshake(). Without this, the protocol has a liveness failure under any realistic enrollment rate.

candidate.title: Blind scope commitments against bitmask brute-force
candidate.description: scopeCommitment in AgentPolicy is Poseidon(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment). The permissionBitmask is 64 bits with cumulative encoding, yielding at most ~2^16 realistic combinations. An observer who knows the credentialCommitment (public at enrollment) can brute-force the bitmask from the public scopeCommitment, breaking scope privacy. Replace with Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt) where blindingSalt is a fresh private input. Update the Delegation circuit's chain-linking check to use the same 3-ary hash. This is a straightforward privacy fix with minimal constraint cost (~800 additional constraints for one extra Poseidon input).

candidate.title: IETF Internet-Draft: Bolyra Mutual Authentication Protocol
candidate.description: Write a complete Internet-Draft (draft-bolyra-mutual-auth-00) with RFC 2119 normative language covering: terminology (identity commitment, credential commitment, scope commitment, nullifier), protocol overview with MSC diagrams for enrollment and handshake flows, wire format using CBOR (RFC 8949) for proof payloads and public signals, circuit specification references, and Security Considerations section addressing the known phantom-delegatee and stale-root issues. This is the foundational document that all other standards work depends on — without it, interop claims are aspirational.
